description: Quality and correctness rules for offline three.js WebGL2 3D games built from the web-components-3d Lego catalog. Covers the local-importmap-only loading contract (no CDN, no network, runs under file://), draw-call and DPR budgets, delta-time movement, dispose-on-teardown and object pooling, mobile light limits, hand-rolled physics versus rigid bodies, audio unlock on first user gesture, and WebGL2-not-WebGPU. These rules are for 3D games only and must not leak into productivity mini-apps.

guidelines.rule: The importmap MUST be LOCAL and relative only. Map the bare specifier 'three' to './resources/three/three.module.min.js' and the PREFIX 'three/addons/' to './resources/three/addons/'. NEVER reference any https:// URL, CDN, unpkg, jsdelivr, esm.sh, skypack, or es-module-shims. Any 'https://' inside the importmap or in an import statement is an instant failure.
guidelines.why: These games ship as static Hub bundles and run under file:// inside WKWebView, which is not a secure context and has no network guarantee. A CDN importmap renders a blank canvas offline. The local prefix mapping for 'three/addons/' is mandatory so addon-to-addon cross-imports (EffectComposer pulling Pass, RenderPass, shaders) resolve locally instead of hitting the network.
examples: <script type="importmap">{ "imports": { "three": "./resources/three/three.module.min.js", "three/addons/": "./resources/three/addons/" } }</script>
Game code uses canonical specifiers: import * as THREE from 'three'; import { OrbitControls } from 'three/addons/controls/OrbitControls.js';
WRONG: "three": "https://unpkg.com/three@0.160.0/build/three.module.js" - fails the local-importmap gate
WRONG: <script src="https://cdn.jsdelivr.net/npm/es-module-shims"> - native importmaps are guaranteed on the target webviews, do not add a shim

guidelines.rule: The importmap block MUST appear in <head> BEFORE the first module <script>. Place it ahead of any import so the resolver sees the mappings when the first module loads.
guidelines.why: An importmap declared after a module script has already started resolving is ignored, and 'Failed to resolve module specifier three' kills the whole scene. Order is load-bearing, not cosmetic.
examples: Head order: <meta charset> then <style> then <script type="importmap"> then <script type="module" src="./game.js">
Never inject the importmap from JS after DOMContentLoaded - it is too late

guidelines.rule: Use the WebGL2 path only. Construct three.js WebGLRenderer (never WebGPURenderer). If TSL or node materials are used they MUST compile to the GLSL/WebGL backend. GPGPU float-texture sims must capability-check EXT_color_buffer_float and degrade gracefully when absent.
guidelines.why: file:// is not a secure context, so even Safari 26 silently drops WebGPU - WebGPURenderer produces a permanently blank canvas in the shipped bundle. WebGLRenderer is the only renderer that survives the offline file:// target. Float-texture GPGPU effects (flow field, boids, reaction-diffusion) crash without the float color-buffer extension on weaker mobile GPUs.
examples: const renderer = new THREE.WebGLRenderer({ antialias: true, powerPreference: 'high-performance' });
WRONG: new THREE.WebGPURenderer(...) - blank under file://
if (!renderer.capabilities.isWebGL2) { /* show a friendly message, do not crash */ }
const floatOK = renderer.getContext().getExtension('EXT_color_buffer_float'); if (!floatOK) useCPUFallback();

guidelines.rule: Keep the scene under 100 draw calls. Merge static geometry, use InstancedMesh for repeated objects (obstacles, asteroids, projectiles, creeps, particles), and share materials. Watch renderer.info.render.calls and treat it as a budget, not a number to discover after shipping.
guidelines.why: Each draw call is CPU-bound submission overhead; on mobile GPUs hundreds of individual meshes drop the game below 30fps even when the polygon count is tiny. Instancing draws thousands of identical objects in one call. The web-components-3d systems Lego (instanced-obstacle-field, instanced-asteroid-field, instanced-projectiles) exist specifically so genre code never spawns one Mesh per entity.
examples: const field = new THREE.InstancedMesh(geo, mat, 500); // one draw call for 500 obstacles
Reuse a single shared material across all instances; do not clone a material per object
Log renderer.info.render.calls in dev and assert it stays under 100
Merge static level geometry with BufferGeometryUtils.mergeGeometries at load time

guidelines.rule: Clamp devicePixelRatio. Call renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2)) and re-apply on resize. Never render at the raw DPR of a 3x Retina phone.
guidelines.why: A 3x DPR phone renders 9x the pixels of a 1x display; on a fragment-heavy 3D scene that is the difference between 60fps and a slideshow. Clamping to 2 keeps the image crisp while capping the pixel budget. This is the single highest-ROI mobile performance fix.
examples: renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2));
On resize: renderer.setSize(w, h); renderer.setPixelRatio(Math.min(window.devicePixelRatio || 1, 2));
WRONG: renderer.setPixelRatio(window.devicePixelRatio) with no clamp

guidelines.rule: All movement, physics, timers, and animation MUST be delta-time based. Use a fixed-timestep accumulator: accumulate real elapsed seconds, step update(dt) at a fixed dt (e.g. 1/60), clamp dt to avoid a spiral of death after a tab stall, and interpolate the render. Never advance state by a constant per-frame amount.
guidelines.why: Frame rate varies across devices (30, 60, 120Hz) and stalls when a tab backgrounds. Per-frame movement makes a 120Hz phone run the game twice as fast as a 60Hz one and teleports everything after a stall. Fixed-timestep with a clamped accumulator gives identical, deterministic motion on every device.
examples: position.x += speed * dt; // not position.x += speed
acc += Math.min(rawElapsed, 0.1); while (acc >= STEP) { update(STEP); acc -= STEP; }
Clamp the raw frame delta to ~0.1s so a backgrounded tab does not produce one giant catch-up step
core.three-game-loop already implements this accumulator - import it instead of hand-rolling rAF

guidelines.rule: The render loop MUST increment window.__threeFrameCount once per rendered frame. Initialize it to 0 and bump it inside the animation callback right after renderer.render(...).
guidelines.why: The macOS InstalledMiniAppRenderProbe reads window.__threeFrameCount to confirm the WebGL scene is actually rendering frames (frameCount greater than 0), not just sitting on a static first frame or a silently failed context. Omitting this counter makes a working game fail the render probe and get flagged unplayable.
examples: window.__threeFrameCount = 0; // at startup
function animate() { update(dt); renderer.render(scene, camera); window.__threeFrameCount++; }
renderer.setAnimationLoop(animate); // the loop that bumps the counter

guidelines.rule: Dispose every GPU resource on teardown. Geometries, materials, textures, render targets, and the renderer itself must be explicitly disposed when the game ends or the view is torn down. Walk the scene and call .dispose() on geometry, material(s), and any map/texture, then renderer.dispose().
guidelines.why: three.js does not garbage-collect GPU memory. Geometries, textures, and render targets live in WebGL until you dispose them; a game that rebuilds its scene on restart without disposing leaks VRAM until the context is lost and the canvas goes black. Mini-apps get torn down and relaunched repeatedly in the host, so the leak compounds fast.
examples: scene.traverse(o => { o.geometry?.dispose(); const m = o.material; (Array.isArray(m) ? m : [m]).forEach(mat => { mat?.map?.dispose(); mat?.dispose(); }); });
renderTarget.dispose(); composer?.dispose?.(); renderer.dispose();
Remove resize / visibilitychange / pointer listeners in the same dispose() path
core.three-game-loop exposes a dispose() - call it from the host teardown hook

guidelines.rule: Pool particles, projectiles, and short-lived objects. Pre-allocate a fixed pool (or a single InstancedMesh) and recycle entries instead of creating and destroying meshes/geometries every frame or every shot. Route all particles through effects.instanced-particle-pool.
guidelines.why: Allocating a Mesh, geometry, or material per projectile or per particle every frame triggers constant GC pauses and the dispose churn of tjs-008. A fixed pool keeps allocation flat: spawning a bullet just activates a free slot and resets its transform; despawning returns it to the pool. This is what keeps bullet-hell and particle bursts at a steady frame rate.
examples: const pool = []; for (let i=0;i<256;i++) pool.push(makeBullet()); // pre-allocated, hidden until used
spawn(): take a dead slot, set position/velocity, mark alive; despawn(): mark dead, hide, return to pool
Particles: one additive Points or InstancedMesh, update positions in a typed array, never one mesh per spark
Never new THREE.Mesh(...) inside the per-frame update loop

guidelines.rule: Cap active lights at 3 on mobile. Prefer a single DirectionalLight (sun) plus one HemisphereLight (sky/ground fill); bake or fake the rest. Limit shadow-casting to one light and use a blob/baked shadow for everything else. Do not add a PointLight per object.
guidelines.why: Each real-time light multiplies fragment-shader cost, and each shadow-casting light adds a full extra render pass. Four or more dynamic lights with shadows tank mobile GPUs. The scene-rig recipe (1 DirectionalLight + 1 HemisphereLight) plus blob shadows looks great and stays cheap. Per-object PointLights are the classic way an otherwise simple scene drops to single-digit fps.
examples: scene.add(new THREE.DirectionalLight(0xffffff, 2.0)); scene.add(new THREE.HemisphereLight(0xbfd4ff, 0x40402a, 0.6));
Only the DirectionalLight casts shadows; everything else uses effects.blob-shadow
WRONG: attaching a PointLight to each collectible or enemy
core.three-scene-rig already wires the 2-light recipe - reuse it

guidelines.rule: Hand-roll movement and collision unless the genre genuinely needs rigid-body dynamics. Use simple kinematics (capsule sweep, AABB/sphere overlap, raycast ground snap) for runners, racers, platformers, flight, tower defense, and rhythm games. Only pull systems.rapier-physics-world for genres whose fun IS emergent rigid-body physics (stacking, tumbling, ragdoll, knock-down).
guidelines.why: Rapier-compat adds ~1.9MB of embedded wasm to every bundle and a full physics step to every frame. For a lane runner or an on-rails racer that overhead buys nothing - a few lines of kinematic math are smaller, faster, and easier to tune. Reserve the one physics-engine Lego for games where you actually want bodies to collide, topple, and settle unpredictably.
examples: Runner/racer/flight/TD/rhythm: kinematic update + sphere or AABB overlap checks, no engine
Platformer ground snap: a single downward raycast, not a physics body
Stacking / tumbling / domino / ragdoll: import systems.rapier-physics-world (embedded-wasm, offline)
Do not add rapier to a bundle that never calls world.step()

guidelines.rule: Unlock audio on the first real user gesture. AudioContext (and three.js AudioListener / PositionalAudio) boots suspended; on the first tap/click/keydown call audioContext.resume() and play a 1-sample silent buffer, THEN start sound. Always ship a visible tap-to-start / Play control. If audio cannot start, keep the game fully playable and visually intact, just muted.
guidelines.why: iOS and WKWebView require a genuine user gesture to start audio; calling resume() at load does nothing and leaves the game permanently silent. The silent-buffer trick fully unlocks the context on iOS. Gating sound start behind a Play button is the reliable pattern. Audio must never block rendering - a muted game still renders, so degrade gracefully rather than stalling on a suspended context.
examples: playButton.addEventListener('pointerdown', () => { audioCtx.resume(); playSilentSample(audioCtx); startGame(); }, { once: true });
playSilentSample: const b = ctx.createBuffer(1,1,22050); const s = ctx.createBufferSource(); s.buffer=b; s.connect(ctx.destination); s.start(0);
three.js: const listener = new THREE.AudioListener(); camera.add(listener); resume the listener.context on first tap
If audioCtx.state stays 'suspended', continue rendering muted - never block the render loop on audio

guidelines.rule: If the goal is a GAME, ship a COMPLETE PLAYABLE core loop on first load, not just an ambient scene: the player takes an action, sees an immediate consequence, and can reach a win OR lose state by playing. A pretty scene with only idle motion and no interaction is a FAILURE for a game goal.
guidelines.why: 3D builds routinely render a beautiful scene but forget the game (no reachable objective, no working enemies, no win/lose). The taste rubric and render-gate both PASS a blank-but-pretty canvas, so nothing else catches it. Playability is the actual product.
examples: Shooter: enemies spawn + advance, firing spawns projectiles that hit + kill them, score climbs, health to 0 = Game Over
Runner: obstacles approach, a collision ends the run, distance/score climbs
WRONG: a starfield + a ship that only idles, no visible enemies, no way to win or lose

guidelines.rule: Every gameplay entity the player reacts to (enemies, targets, hazards, collectibles) MUST be VISIBLE in the camera view at start or spawn within ~2s, and MUST actually move/respond: enemies path toward or attack the player, projectiles travel and register hits, collectibles vanish + score on pickup. A static, off-screen, or inert entity is a failure.
guidelines.why: Players reported 'I can't see an enemy or evade' — entities were never spawned or placed outside the frame, so there was nothing to play against. If the player cannot SEE and INTERACT with the entities, the game is unplayable no matter how nice the scene.
examples: FPS/dogfight: enemies enter the camera frustum, visibly approach/shoot; crosshair + on-hit feedback confirm hits
Collectathon: gems sit in view on the reachable path, vanish + score on contact
WRONG: enemies spawned behind the camera or never spawned

guidelines.rule: Every control you document (WASD, arrows, mouse-look, click-to-fire, space-to-jump, drag) MUST be wired to a handler that visibly changes the scene the SAME frame it is pressed: add the listeners, update an input state, apply it in the update loop. Mentally test each key — does pressing it produce an immediate on-screen response?
guidelines.why: A frequent failure is keys/buttons with NO attached handler (the evaluator's 'NO HANDLER ATTACHED' finding): the HUD shows controls but nothing happens. Unresponsive controls make the game unplayable even when it renders.
examples: keydown/keyup set input flags; the fixed-step update reads them and moves the player; camera follows
click → pointer-lock; pointermove rotates the view (mouse-look)
WRONG: a 'Jump' button or 'W' key with no addEventListener / no state mutation

guidelines.rule: Wire score/health/collision/timer so the player can ACTUALLY reach the win state by playing well and the lose state by failing — both reachable — and show a clear overlay ('You Win!' / 'Game Over') with a working Restart. Avoid instant-fail, unreachable goals, or no end at all.
guidelines.why: A game with no win/lose, or an unreachable objective, has no stakes and no completion — it reads as a tech demo, not a game. The end states + restart are what make it feel finished and playable.
examples: health to 0 → Game Over overlay + Restart; reach the goal / survive the timer / clear all waves → You Win
WRONG: a score counter that never triggers any end state

guidelines.rule: Aim the camera so the player can SEE what they must react to: enemies approaching, the path/obstacles ahead, collectibles, the goal. First-person: keep threats entering the forward view. Third-person/chase: frame the player plus nearby threats with lookahead. Top-down/orbit: keep the whole play area legible.
guidelines.why: Even with working entities + controls, a camera pointed away from the action (or too close, or not following) hides the gameplay so the player can't react — indistinguishable from broken.
examples: chase camera trails the player with lookahead toward oncoming obstacles
FPS camera at eye height, enemies spawning ahead/around (not behind)
WRONG: a fixed camera on empty sky while the action happens off-screen

guidelines.rule: The instructions / how-to-play / controls panel MUST be collapsible: give it a minimize button that shrinks it to a small round info ('i') icon in the same corner (click the icon to expand), and optionally auto-collapse after ~9 seconds. Prefer the reusable ui.info-panel component (createInfoPanel({title, html, corner, autoCollapseMs})). Keep the score/HUD compact and separate. The instructions panel must NOT permanently cover a large part of the scene.
guidelines.why: Generated instruction boxes routinely cover a third of the screen and break immersion (real user feedback on Labyrinth-3D). A one-click minimize-to-icon keeps the how-to-play available without dominating the view.

guidelines.rule: NEVER use requestPointerLock / pointer-lock for camera or aim control. It does NOT work in the mChatAI+ in-app preview (WKWebView), so a pointer-lock first-person game is UNPLAYABLE there (you cannot look or aim) and also breaks on mobile. Use DRAG-TO-LOOK instead: hold-and-drag the view to rotate (mouse-drag on desktop, touch-drag on mobile); WASD or an on-screen joystick to move; a quick tap/click or an on-screen button or Space to shoot/act. Strongly prefer the controls.fps-controller component (default lookMode 'drag', built-in mobile joystick + action buttons + wall-slide collision) rather than hand-rolling controls.
guidelines.why: Pointer-lock is not granted in the preview WKWebView; many FPS builds render fine but are impossible to play. Drag-look + touch controls work in the preview, on the hub, and on phones.

guidelines.rule: Make 3D games playable on BOTH desktop and mobile. The canvas must fill and resize to the viewport (handle resize + devicePixelRatio). Provide touch controls: drag-to-look, an on-screen movement joystick, and large on-screen action buttons (>= 64px). No pointer-lock, no hover-only interactions, no tiny click targets. Keyboard/mouse still work on desktop. controls.fps-controller and controls.virtual-joystick-3d already provide touch controls.
guidelines.why: Most generated 3D games are desktop-only (WASD+pointer-lock) and unplayable on a phone or in the preview. A drag-look + on-screen-joystick scheme is playable everywhere with no code branches on device.

guidelines.rule: For ships, enemies, characters, and props, PREFER the ready-made low-poly model builders (models.lowpoly-vehicles-3d, models.lowpoly-characters-3d, models.lowpoly-props-3d) over bare boxes/spheres or hand-built geometry. They are buildX(THREE, opts:{color,scale}) -> THREE.Group (THREE passed in). E.g. buildDrone/buildSlime/buildAlienShip for enemies, buildRobot for a player, buildSpaceship/buildKart for vehicles, buildTree/buildRock/buildCrate/buildCoin/buildGem/buildTurret for props. Tint via opts.color. This makes games look like real 3D instead of cubes. For ANIMATED enemies/creatures (the ones that must move), prefer models.procedural-creatures-3d (buildCreature + animateCreature) over the static lowpoly-characters builders -- see tjs-018. For ground, use systems.heightmap-terrain-3d (smooth) or systems.voxel-chunk-3d (blocky) rather than a flat plane.
guidelines.why: Generated 3D games default to plain boxes for enemies/props, which looks unfinished. Reusing the model builders gives recognizable, good-looking low-poly models for free.

guidelines.rule: Animated characters and creatures MUST visibly animate every frame, not sit in a T-pose. Prefer models.procedural-creatures-3d (buildCreature + animateCreature(group,{clip,t})) for rigged creatures with ZERO asset files, or models.gltf-model-player-3d (AnimationMixer) for GLB assets. Drive the pose from the fixed-timestep clock so a walking enemy's legs move, an attack swings, and hit/death play. A static, un-animated character reads as broken.
guidelines.why: Generated 3D games place a model and never animate it, so enemies glide like statues and players report 'nothing is alive.' Procedural rigs (the World-of-ClaudeCraft pattern) animate by rotating nested pivot groups each frame with zero asset files, fully offline. This is the difference between a diorama and a game (tjs-013/tjs-014).
examples: const g = buildCreature(THREE,{family:'wolf'}); scene.add(g); // each frame: animateCreature(g,{clip:enemy.state, t:(enemy.animTime+=dt)})
cycle enemy.state through 'idle' -> 'walk' -> 'attack' as your AI decides; animateCreature reads it
WRONG: add a character model and never call any per-frame animation

guidelines.rule: Synthesize all sound effects procedurally with WebAudio -- do NOT ship audio files. Route SFX and positional audio through effects.audio-manager-3d (oscillator+gain+filter ADSR presets for shoot/hit/explosion/pickup/coin/levelup; THREE.PositionalAudio for spatialized sources). Attach it to the game loop so the existing first-tap unlock is reused. If audio cannot start, keep the game fully playable and visually intact, just muted.
guidelines.why: Audio files mean network fetches or large base64 blobs that bloat the offline bundle; a procedural WebAudio synth (the World-of-ClaudeCraft pattern) produces convincing SFX with zero bytes and zero network, and works under file://. One AudioManager owns the listener and pools sources. Never block the render loop on a suspended context (tjs-012).
examples: const audio = new AudioManager3D({camera}); audio.attachTo(loop); // unlock reused via loop.setAudioContext
on a hit: audio.play('hit', {position: enemy.position});
WRONG: <audio src='explosion.mp3'> or fetching a sound file -- breaks offline and bloats the bundle

guidelines.rule: Separate gameplay state from rendering: keep the simulation in a pure, DOM-free, deterministic module stepped at a fixed timestep -- sim.step(state, input, dt) -> state -- and a separate render(state) that reads it and updates three.js objects. The sim must not touch the DOM, the renderer, or wall-clock time directly. Drive both from core.three-game-loop's fixed-step update / interpolated render.
guidelines.why: Mixing gameplay math into the render callback makes motion frame-rate-dependent (tjs-006) and untestable. A pure dt-stepped sim is deterministic across 30/60/120Hz devices, can be unit-tested headless without a WebGL context, and makes the win/lose wiring (tjs-016) auditable. This is the testability core of the World-of-ClaudeCraft shared-sim pattern, applied to a single offline client (these bundles are offline single-file apps; no server is involved).
examples: function step(state, input, dt){ /* pure: positions, health, score, collisions */ return state; }
loop: onUpdate=(dt)=>{ state=step(state,input,dt); }; onRender=(a)=>{ syncMeshes(state); }
WRONG: mutating mesh.position and reading Date.now() inside the same rAF callback that also computes damage

guidelines.rule: Use the shared camera rig; do not hand-roll a bespoke camera per game. Instantiate controls.camera-rig-3d and pick a mode (chase / follow / orbit / rise / first-person); call rig.setTarget(player); rig.update(dt) each frame. Apply effects.screen-shake AFTER the rig sets the base transform so the shake composes instead of fighting the camera. For first-person games use FIRST_PERSON mode + controls.fps-controller.
guidelines.why: Hand-rolled cameras are the top cause of the 'camera pointed away from the action' failure (tjs-017) and of cameras that clip, jitter, or never follow. A shared rig with smoothed look-ahead keeps threats and the path in frame on every genre and converges the catalog on one verified camera shape.
examples: const rig = new CameraRig({camera, mode: CHASE, target: player, lookAhead: 4}); // each frame: rig.update(dt); shake.update(dt, camera);
top-down RPG: mode FOLLOW; flight: mode CHASE with high lookAhead; showpiece: mode ORBIT
WRONG: manually setting camera.position from scratch every frame with no damping or look-ahead

guidelines.rule: Ground every entity cheaply: keep exactly ONE shadow-casting light (the DirectionalLight sun) and give other entities a fake contact shadow via effects.blob-shadow (a soft dark disc under the entity, pooled with InstancedMesh for crowds). Do not enable castShadow on multiple lights or per-entity.
guidelines.why: Each shadow-casting light adds a full render pass and tanks mobile GPUs (tjs-010). A blob shadow costs one transparent disc and makes characters read as grounded rather than floating, keeping the scene inside the draw-call (tjs-004) and light (tjs-010) budgets.
examples: const shadows = new BlobShadowPool({scene, capacity: 64}); // per entity: const id=shadows.claim(); shadows.place(id, enemyMesh, groundY)
only the sun casts: dirLight.castShadow = true; everything else uses a blob
WRONG: enemyLight.castShadow = true on a PointLight attached to each enemy

guidelines.rule: Import three.js with a STATIC top-level `import * as THREE from 'three';` (resolved by the `<script type=importmap>` you already emit). NEVER use a dynamic `const THREE = await import('three')`, and NEVER wrap it in a 'load from CDN' try/catch fallback. Dynamic import() of a bare importmap specifier does NOT resolve in the in-app WKWebView preview under file:// (the page shows 'Could not load three.js' even though the vendored copy is present), while static imports work. Addons likewise: `import { OrbitControls } from 'three/addons/controls/OrbitControls.js';`.
guidelines.why: Observed 2026-06-17: a real-three.js floating-island build rendered blank with 'Could not load three.js from the CDN' purely because it used `await import('three')`; the identical scene with a static `import * as THREE from 'three'` renders. The vendored three.module.min.js was present and valid — only the import STYLE differed. Every shipped working 3D scene uses the static form.
examples: GOOD: <script type=importmap>{...}</script> then <script type=module> import * as THREE from 'three'; ...
BAD: let THREE; try { THREE = await import('three'); } catch { showError('Could not load three.js from the CDN'); }

guidelines.rule: First-person / pointer-look 3D: NEVER use `requestPointerLock` and NEVER gate the game start on it — Pointer Lock is dead in the in-app WKWebView preview (and on mobile), so a 'Click to play' that calls requestPointerLock() silently fails and the game never starts. (1) START: a 'Click to play' / canvas click MUST immediately start the game (hide the overlay, begin the loop) unconditionally — never inside or awaiting a pointer-lock request. (2) LOOK: use DELTA-accumulating DRAG-look — on pointerdown set dragging=true; on pointermove while dragging, `yaw -= e.movementX * sens; pitch -= e.movementY * sens` (use movementX/Y deltas, NOT absolute cursor position) so turning is UNBOUNDED (the cursor hitting the window edge must not stop rotation); on pointerup stop. Clamp PITCH to ±(Math.PI/2 - 0.02); NEVER clamp YAW. Provide touch drag + on-screen joystick on mobile. Prefer the vendored `./web-components-3d/controls/` fps-controller, which already does unbounded drag-look + wall-slide.
guidelines.why: Two real failures, 2026-06-20: (a) VoxelCraft 'Click to play' did nothing — its menu click called requestPointerLock(), which is rejected in the WKWebView preview, so the game stayed on the menu. (b) Catacombs 'I can't fully turn around' — drag-look was bounded by the window edge. Pointer Lock is NOT the fix (it's dead here, per AIHarness's own scaffold directive); delta-accumulating drag-look that doesn't gate the start is. Contradicts the earlier wrong version of this rule.
examples: GOOD: menu.addEventListener('click', () => { menu.hidden = true; started = true; }); // start is UNCONDITIONAL, no requestPointerLock

GOOD: cv.addEventListener('pointerdown', e=>{drag=true}); cv.addEventListener('pointermove', e=>{ if(!drag) return; yaw -= e.movementX*sens; pitch = clamp(pitch - e.movementY*sens, -1.55, 1.55); }); cv.addEventListener('pointerup', ()=>{drag=false});
BAD: menu.addEventListener('click', () => canvas.requestPointerLock()); // dead in WKWebView → click-to-play never starts
BAD: look tied to absolute cursor X/Y — turning stops at the screen edge

guidelines.rule: An explorable / first-person 3D scene must be PLAYABLE-bright: include an AmbientLight or HemisphereLight at intensity >= ~0.6 so the player can see walls, floor, and enemies. Atmospheric 'dark' themes (catacombs, dungeon, bunker, night) STILL need this baseline — a ~0.3 hemisphere plus a couple of local torch PointLights leaves the scene too dark to navigate. Avoid near-black floor/wall materials; give surfaces a mid-tone color so the fill light reads.
guidelines.why: Observed 2026-06-20 (Catacombs): HemisphereLight intensity 0.32 + 2 torch PointLights -> user reported 'quite dark and hard to see.' Mood is fine, but the player must be able to navigate; brighter ambient + non-black materials keeps it atmospheric AND playable.
examples: GOOD: scene.add(new THREE.HemisphereLight(0xb0c4de, 0x202028, 0.7)); plus a directional/torch for shape
BAD: HemisphereLight(..., 0.3) as the ONLY fill - corridors render near-black

guidelines.rule: First-person WEAPON VIEWMODEL must stay SMALL and out of the way. It should occupy at most the lower ~1/4 to 1/3 of the screen, anchored toward the BOTTOM and slightly to one side (e.g. lower-right) -- NEVER centered and filling the view. In three.js, parent the weapon to the camera and place it low/forward/to-the-side (roughly position (0.25, -0.35, -0.6) with a small scale) plus a subtle idle/walk bob; keep the upper-center of the screen CLEAR so approaching enemies, the crosshair, and the corridor ahead stay fully visible. A giant weapon mesh that covers most of the viewport is the single most common thing that ruins these FPS builds -- size it like a real game's viewmodel (a sliver at the bottom), not a hero prop.

Make 3D / FPS / driving games genuinely playable on MOBILE with touch. Detect a coarse pointer (matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')) and show on-screen controls: a LEFT virtual joystick for movement (prefer controls.virtual-joystick-3d + controls.input-manager-3d), RIGHT-side drag-to-look (accumulated movementX/Y, never requestPointerLock), and a FIRE/action button (>=44px, bottom-right). The SAME delta-time movement code drives desktop keys and touch. Clamp devicePixelRatio<=2 and lower the entity/particle/draw-call budget on coarse-pointer devices for 60fps. Lay the canvas + HUD out for portrait AND landscape and handle resize/orientationchange. The game must be completable with touch alone -- no keyboard, mouse, or hover required.

NEVER spawn the player (or vehicle) in empty air. Before the loop starts, build a SOLID ground/terrain mesh that covers the ENTIRE walkable/drivable area, then place the player ON it: raycast straight down from high above the spawn XZ with a THREE.Raycaster (direction (0,-1,0)) against the ground/collidable meshes, take the first hit, and set player.position.y = hit.point.y + playerHalfHeight. If the genre has no flat plane (procedural island, voxel world, height-map), write a getGroundY(x,z) sampler that returns the surface height and seed spawn from it; do NOT hardcode y=0. Every frame, snap or gravity-settle the player onto the ground so they cannot drift off the edge into the void. A 'free-fall into a blue void' or a player floating over geometry is a spawn-with-no-ground bug, not a feature.
guidelines.why: Real failures (Island Explorer, Floating Island, Sundive): the player spawned at y=0 / a fixed height with only procedural geometry in negative-Y or a cone-shaped island below, so walking past the island radius left the player over empty gradient sky with nothing underneath -- a free-fall into blue void. The fix is to validate ground at spawn (raycast down or sample a height function) and keep snapping to it, never trusting a hardcoded y.
examples: const ray = new THREE.Raycaster(new THREE.Vector3(x, 1000, z), new THREE.Vector3(0,-1,0)); const hit = ray.intersectObjects(groundMeshes, true)[0]; player.position.set(x, (hit ? hit.point.y : 0) + PLAYER_HALF_H, z);
Procedural island: function getGroundY(x,z){ /* sample island rings / height field */ return surfaceY; } spawn at (0, getGroundY(0,2.6)+PLAYER_HALF_H, 2.6);
Every frame: player.position.y = getGroundY(player.position.x, player.position.z) + PLAYER_HALF_H; (or apply gravity + ground-clamp)
WRONG: player.position.set(0, 0, 2.6) with island geometry only in negative Y -- walk off the rim and free-fall into the blue void

Build the ground BEFORE the camera/loop: a flat terrain via systems.heightmap-terrain-3d, voxel floor via systems.voxel-chunk-3d, or at minimum a large THREE.Mesh(PlaneGeometry) collidable that spans the play area

guidelines.rule: Render ENOUGH world around the player that MOTION IS PERCEIVABLE. (1) Texture the ground: give it a repeating grid/checker/noise texture or per-tile color variation -- never a single flat untextured color, or movement looks frozen. (2) Place LANDMARKS near spawn: trees, rocks, buildings, posts, hills within ~10-40 units in several directions so the player sees things slide past as they move; do NOT rely on far-only or sky-only objects. (3) Add a HORIZON/skybox plus distance fog: set scene.fog (THREE.Fog or FogExp2) and the camera far plane so the world fades into a horizon instead of an infinite empty gradient, and verify geometry actually exists out to the fog distance. (4) Give the player a visible ground-contact shadow (effects.blob-shadow) and optional motion dust/trail so vertical position and speed read clearly. A scene that is just a sky gradient + one cone has no motion reference and feels like free-fall even when grounded.
guidelines.why: Real failure (Floating Island): pure procedural wireframe with an icy-blue gradient that extended to the horizon regardless of geometry below, minimal landmarks, no ground texture, no shadow -- the player could move in any direction with zero anchoring feedback and could not tell their vertical position. Ground texture + near landmarks + a real horizon/fog + a contact shadow give the parallax and reference that make motion legible.
examples: const t = makeGridTexture(); t.wrapS=t.wrapT=THREE.RepeatWrapping; t.repeat.set(64,64); groundMat.map=t; // ground slides visibly underfoot
scene.fog = new THREE.FogExp2(0x9fc4e8, 0.012); camera.far = 600; camera.updateProjectionMatrix(); // world fades to a horizon, not infinite void
Scatter ~30 buildX(THREE) trees/rocks/crates within 40 units of spawn so something passes by as the player walks
effects.blob-shadow under the player so ground contact and height read at a glance
WRONG: a single cone island in an unbounded gradient sky with no ground texture and no nearby props -- moving looks like nothing is happening

guidelines.rule: COLLISION is mandatory in explorable / open-world / driving scenes -- boundary clamping is NOT collision. Every solid object (walls, trees, buildings, rocks, fences, terrain slopes) MUST have a collider, and you MUST sweep the player AND vehicles against those colliders so you cannot walk or drive THROUGH solid geometry. Use a capsule (player) or AABB/box (vehicle) and test the proposed next position before committing it: if the swept body overlaps a collider, slide along the surface (project the motion onto the wall tangent) instead of clamping to a box or stopping dead. Prefer the collision Lego (controls.fps-controller's built-in wall-slide, systems.kinematic-body-3d) rather than hand-rolling; reserve systems.rapier-physics-world for genres whose fun is rigid-body dynamics (tjs-011). Do NOT ship a world where structures are pure visual faces the player phases through.
guidelines.why: Real failures (Sundive, Floating Island): both used only simple clamp-to-rectangle bounds with no geometry checks, so buildings, trees, and walls were pure visual faces the player walked straight through, and there was no ray/sweep validating the player stayed on the surface. An explorable world with no collision feels broken and lets the player escape the playable area. Capsule/AABB sweep + wall-slide is the minimum bar.
examples: Player: import controls.fps-controller (capsule + wall-slide) OR systems.kinematic-body-3d; feed it the collider list so movement sweeps and slides, never tunnels
Hand-rolled sweep: const next = pos.clone().add(move); if (!collides(next, PLAYER_RADIUS)) pos.copy(next); else pos.copy(slideAlongWall(pos, move, hitNormal));
Vehicle: AABB or oriented box swept against building/wall AABBs each step; on overlap, kill the into-wall velocity component and keep the tangent
Give each building/tree/rock a collider (Box3 / sphere / mesh) registered in a collidables array the sweep iterates
WRONG: clamp player.x/z to [-84,84] with no per-object checks -- the player drives through every building

guidelines.rule: The look / camera controller MUST persist across state changes -- it must keep working when the player enters or exits a vehicle, opens a menu, respawns, or changes mode. Drive the camera from a user-controlled yaw/pitch offset that survives mode switches; do NOT hard-lock the camera to a vehicle's heading and silently drop look input on entering drive mode. On every mode change, RE-BIND (or keep bound) the pointer/touch drag-look and joystick-look handlers so look is never dead. In drive mode, keep an independent free-look offset (left stick / WASD steers; right stick / mouse-drag still rotates the camera around the car) so the player can look around while driving. Use the shared controls.camera-rig-3d (tjs-021) and apply the persistent look offset on top of the rig's base transform.
guidelines.why: Real failure (Sundive Hover Courier): drag-look only updated yaw/pitch when mode==='foot'; in drive mode the camera was hard-locked to car.heading with NO pointer handling, so entering the vehicle made look input completely dead. The fix is a look offset that persists across modes and handlers that stay bound (or are re-bound) on every state switch, with free-look decoupled from steering in vehicles.
examples: Keep cam.yawOffset / cam.pitchOffset as user state; foot AND drive both apply: cam.yaw = (mode==='drive' ? car.heading : player.yaw) + cam.yawOffset;
toggleVehicle(): do NOT remove the drag-look listeners; both modes read the same lookDelta(dx,dy) -> cam.yawOffset -= dx*sens; cam.pitchOffset = clamp(cam.pitchOffset - dy*sens, -1.3, 1.3);
Drive mode: left stick / arrows steer the car, right-stick / mouse-drag rotates cam.yawOffset around the car independently
WRONG: if (mode==='foot') { yaw -= dx } else { cam.yaw = car.heading } -- look is dead the instant you enter the vehicle

guidelines.rule: On MOBILE, an open-world / explorable / driving game MUST show BOTH on-screen sticks at once -- a LEFT movement joystick AND a RIGHT-side drag-to-look zone (or right look-stick) -- plus the action buttons (enter/exit vehicle, jump, fire, brake) sized >=64px. Do NOT ship only the left move stick: a right-look control that is styled display:none, only enabled in foot mode, or never created leaves mobile players unable to turn the camera. Detect a coarse pointer (matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)')) and reveal both sticks for foot AND drive modes; track a SEPARATE pointer id per stick so move + look work simultaneously with two thumbs. The right stick/zone updates the camera yaw/pitch offset (tjs-031), independent of steering. Reinforces tjs-027.
guidelines.why: Real failure (Sundive): #bR (the right look stick) was styled display:none and never shown, and drag-look was foot-mode-only and disabled in vehicles, so mobile players got a left MOVE stick only and could not look around at all despite the instructions saying 'drag to look.' Both sticks must be present and dual-touch capable in every mode.
examples: if (matchMedia('(pointer: coarse)').matches) { moveStick.hidden=false; lookStick.hidden=false; } // BOTH, in foot AND drive
Per-stick pointer id: on pointerdown over the left zone capture id->'move', over the right zone id->'look'; pointermove routes by id so two thumbs work at once
Right look stick / drag zone updates cam.yawOffset/pitchOffset (never requestPointerLock); buttons (enter/exit, jump, fire) are >=64px in the corners
WRONG: #bR { display:none } or only enabling the right stick when mode==='foot' -- mobile players cannot turn

guidelines.rule: Give the player immediate, continuous MOTION FEEDBACK so they always know they are moving and how fast. Show a live readout that updates every frame -- a speed/MPH/units-per-second HUD for driving, a compass/heading or minimap for exploration -- and reinforce it visually: ground detail (texture, grid lines, scattered props from tjs-029) visibly streaming past, plus optional speed-scaled effects (subtle FOV widening, motion dust, screen-edge speed lines, engine pitch rising with speed). The feedback must scale with actual velocity, not be a static label. If the speed readout never changes or no ground detail passes by, the player cannot tell movement from a frozen frame.
guidelines.why: Closes the loop on tjs-028/tjs-029: even with a real ground and nearby props, players need an explicit signal that input is producing motion. A speed HUD plus passing ground detail (and engine pitch / dust for vehicles) makes movement obviously responsive; without it, slow or smooth movement over a sparse world reads as stuck.
examples: Driving HUD: speedEl.textContent = Math.round(car.speed * 3.6) + ' km/h'; updated in the render callback every frame
Exploration: a small compass or minimap dot that rotates/translates with the player so heading and position are legible
Speed-scaled feel: camera.fov = baseFov + Math.min(car.speed*0.4, 12); camera.updateProjectionMatrix(); plus engine osc pitch = base + speed*k
Ground texture + scattered props (tjs-029) visibly stream past at velocity so motion is self-evident
WRONG: a fixed 'Speed: 0' label and an untextured ground -- the player cannot tell if they are moving

guidelines.rule: DRIVING / VEHICLE games specifically: build the drivable surface as a real road/terrain mesh with colliders (tjs-028, tjs-030) and keep the car ON it -- raycast down from the car each step to sit it on the road and orient to the slope; do not let it sink, float, or drive off into void. Steering and throttle MUST be delta-time based (tjs-006): accumulate speed from throttle with drag/friction, turn the heading by steerInput * turnRate * speedFactor * dt (slower turning at low speed), and apply velocity along the heading. Sweep the car's box against world colliders and walls (tjs-030) so it cannot pass through buildings. Keep a chase camera (controls.camera-rig-3d, CHASE mode) trailing the car with look-ahead, AND preserve free-look (tjs-031) so the player can glance around while driving. Provide a speed HUD (tjs-033) and, on mobile, both sticks (tjs-032: left stick steers/accelerates, right stick looks).
guidelines.why: Driving builds combine every open-world failure at once -- the car spawns in air, phases through buildings, the camera locks to heading and kills look, and there is no speed feedback. This rule binds the ground-validation, collision, persistent-look, mobile dual-stick, and motion-feedback rules into one driving-specific contract so a generated driving game is grounded, solid, controllable, and legibly fast.
examples: Each step: down-raycast from car.position to the road mesh -> set car.y = hit.point.y + rideHeight and tilt to hit.face.normal (slope orientation)
car.speed += (throttle*accel - car.speed*drag) * dt; car.heading += steer * turnRate * Math.min(1, car.speed/turnSpeedRef) * dt; car.position.addScaledVector(headingVec, car.speed*dt);
Sweep the car AABB/oriented-box against building+wall colliders (tjs-030); on hit, cancel the into-wall component and keep sliding
const rig = new CameraRig({camera, mode: CHASE, target: car, lookAhead: 6}); apply cam.yawOffset/pitchOffset on top for free-look while driving
WRONG: car at fixed y that floats over hills, clamped to a rectangle, camera welded to car.heading, no speed readout

guidelines.rule: EVERY per-frame system function you define MUST be CALLED from the single requestAnimationFrame loop (animate()) — or from a fixed-step update() that the loop drives. Defining updateGates / updateRocks / updateObstacles / updateEnemies / checkCollisions / updateHUD and then forgetting to invoke it is the single most common silent failure in these builds: the function exists, the diff looks complete and even reads as 'done', but the entities it drives never move, re-seat, scroll, or collide. Before you finish, scan your OWN code: for every function named update*/tick*/step*/check*/advance*, confirm there is an actual call site inside the frame loop. A per-frame function that is defined but never called is a bug — wire it in or delete it. Never trust that a build is complete because the functions are present; verify the call sites exist.
guidelines.why: Repeated real failure: an iteration added updateGates() and updateRocks() but never called them in animate(), so the terrain scrolled while the gates and rocks stayed frozen in world space and appeared to hang in mid-air. The delivery check even flagged the functions as 'unwired', but the fix did not land. Presence of a function is not evidence it runs. This is the 3D analogue of dead-code that passes a glance but fails in play.
examples: function animate(){ requestAnimationFrame(animate); const dt=clock.getDelta(); updateTerrain(dt); updateProps(dt); updatePlayer(dt); checkCollisions(); updateHUD(); renderer.render(scene,camera); window.__threeFrameCount++; }
Self-check before finishing: grep your code for /function (update|tick|step|check)\w+/ and confirm each name also appears as a CALL inside animate() (or a fixed-step update it feeds)
WRONG: define updateGates(){...} and updateRocks(){...} but animate() only calls updateTerrain() — the gates and rocks never move and detach from the scrolling ground

guidelines.rule: Props resting on a SCROLLING or PROCEDURALLY-REGENERATED ground must be re-seated EVERY frame from the SAME height sampler that shapes the terrain. In endless runners, downhill/descent, and flight-over-terrain, the ground moves toward the player or is rebuilt continuously; every object on it — gates, rocks, trees, obstacles, collectibles, flags, checkpoints — must have BOTH its along-track position (advance/recycle its z as the world scrolls) AND its vertical seat (y = heightAt(x, z) + objectHalfHeight) recomputed each frame using the exact heightAt(x,z) / getGroundY(x,z) function used for the terrain vertices. Seat the object ON the surface: a gate's pole bottoms and a rock's base sit AT the sampled height offset by the object's OWN half-height — never an arbitrary constant like +4. Placing a prop once at build time and not updating it makes it stay fixed in world space while the terrain flows underneath, so it visibly detaches and floats. This re-seating belongs in a per-frame updateProps() that tjs-035 requires you to actually call.
guidelines.why: Direct cause of the 'gates hanging in the air' bug: gates were positioned once at build with a fixed +4 vertical offset and were never repositioned as the terrain scrolled, so they floated above and behind the moving surface. Sampling the shared heightAt() every frame for both scroll and seat is what keeps props glued to the ground the player is actually skiing/running over.
examples: updateProps(dt){ worldZ += speed*dt; for(const p of props){ p.position.z = p.baseZ + worldZ; if(p.position.z > recycleZ){ p.baseZ -= trackLen; p.userData.x = randX(); } p.position.y = heightAt(p.userData.x, p.position.z) + p.userData.seat; p.position.x = p.userData.x; } }
Seat exactly on surface: a slalom gate's group origin at heightAt(x,z) so the pole bottoms touch the snow (seat = 0 for base-anchored geometry), not heightAt+4
Use the SAME heightAt(x,z) for terrain vertices AND props so they can never disagree
WRONG: gate.position.set(x, heightAt(x,z)+4, 0) at build time only, with no per-frame update — it floats and detaches as the slope scrolls

guidelines.rule: DOWNHILL / DESCENT games (skiing, snowboarding, sledding, downhill runner, wingsuit, luge) must make the world visibly FALL AWAY from the camera — not read as rolling hills on a level plane. (1) Give the terrain a real net downgrade steep enough to SEE; the fall-line drop must dominate the surface noise, or a slope buried under ripples looks flat. Make the descent gradient clearly steeper than a gentle ramp (exaggerate it for readability — this is a game, not a survey). (2) Pitch the chase camera DOWN the fall line so the run descends toward a HIGH horizon and the slope fills the LOWER portion of the frame; a level, eye-height camera kills the sense of dropping even when the mesh technically slopes. (3) Place fog and the horizon so the descent recedes into distance ahead and below. If a playtester says 'it does not feel like I am going downhill', the grade is too shallow OR the camera is too level — fix BOTH, do not just add more noise.
guidelines.why: Reported failure: a ski run had more terrain ripples but still did not feel like dropping down a mountain, because the net slope was gentle and the camera looked roughly level. Sense of descent comes from a steep readable fall-line PLUS a camera aimed down it so the world drops toward a high horizon — surface noise alone reads as bumps on flat ground.
examples: Terrain: y = -GRADE*forwardDist + noise(x,z), with GRADE large enough that the drop is obvious (the descent term dwarfs the noise amplitude)

Camera down the fall line: place it above/behind the player and camera.lookAt a point well BELOW and ahead (aim at heightAt(aheadX, aheadZ) minus a drop bias) so the horizon sits high and the slope fills the lower frame
Horizon + fog ahead-and-below so the run recedes into the distance as it falls
WRONG: flat plane + rolling noise + a level eye-height camera — reads as driving over gentle hills, not descending a mountain

guidelines.rule: NEVER strand the player on an infinite 'Loading…' spinner. If three.js fails to load OR scene init throws, the loading overlay MUST be removed and a clear, visible error shown instead. Wrap init() in try/catch: on catch, hide the loader AND display an error message. Remove the loader on the FIRST successfully rendered frame (gate it on the animation loop actually running once), NOT on a blind setTimeout that fires even when init hung or threw. A locally vendored three.js has no real 'CDN fallback' — do NOT fabricate a multi-URL fallback list of three identical local paths; load the one local path, and if window.THREE is still undefined afterward, surface the error rather than silently retrying the same path. To the player, a frozen spinner is indistinguishable from a crashed build; an honest error (or a working scene) is mandatory.
guidelines.why: Observed failure: one version showed 'Couldn't start the 3D scene / Three.js could not be loaded' (the offline load contract, tjs-001) while the next version was stuck forever on 'Loading alpine terrain…' because init threw or hung and the overlay was hidden on a fixed timer that could not distinguish success from failure. Gating loader-removal on a real first frame, plus a visible catch, makes broken builds legible instead of a permanent spinner, and stops fake same-path 'fallback' loops.
examples: try { init(); } catch(err){ console.error(err); loadingEl.classList.add('hide'); showError(String(err&&err.message||err)); }
In animate(), the first time it runs: if(!started){ started=true; loadingEl.classList.add('hide'); } — hide the loader on a REAL rendered frame
if (!window.THREE) { showError('Three.js failed to load'); return; } // do not retry the same local path three times

WRONG: setTimeout(()=>loadingEl.classList.add('hide'), 250) that hides the loader whether or not the scene actually started, or a CDNS array of three identical './resources/three/three.min.js' entries
